observations.recommendation: 1. We recommended that the City Accountant submit immediately the DVs, payrolls, ORs, and other liquidation documents of the General Fund from July to December, quarterly FS for all quarters, monthly TBs from January to December, and year- end FS for CY 2022. Henceforth, ensure the submission of accounts and FS within the prescribed periods pursuant to Section 347 of RA No. 7160, Section 70 of NGAS Manual for LGUs, Item 4 of COA Circular No. 2010-01, and Paragraph 7 of COA Circular No. 2009-006.
observations.managementAction: The financial statements for CY 2023 were not submitted within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: 2. We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to demand the settlement of immediate liquidation of all unliquidated cash advances by issuing final demand letters direct to the AOs concerned, otherwise, if no liquidation is made, impose the sanction of withholding the salaries of those who still fail to settle their accounts after due notice.
observations.managementAction: To address this issue, the Accounting Office, to date, has consistently reminded all Accountable Officers (AOs) of their obligation to promptly liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed period. Additionally, a formal demand letter was issued to the previous Local Chief Executive (LCE) for the liquidation of his outstanding cash advance. Some AOs were unable to fully liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed period due to incomplete supporting documents and financial constraints, resulting in delays in the full settlement. Some employees opted to make partial payments.

observations.recommendation:
3. We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to verify the CIP accounts, close to the appropriate infrastructure asset account the cost directly attributable to the completed projects, transfer the recording of all completed projects under the Trust Fund to the General Fund, and subsequently provide the same with depreciation to arrive at an accurate and reliable carrying balance of the PPE, pursuant to Section 50 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I.

4. We further recommended that the City Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reclassify the transactions erroneously recorded under the CIP accounts to their appropriate accounts, and to henceforth, recognize CIP based on progress billings and other acceptable and appropriate supporting documents which will properly substantiate its balance.
observations.managementAction: The City will still comply with the recommendation. The City Accountant will review the CIP accounts and record the necessary adjustments including provision of depreciation upon its transfer to the General Fund. The City Accountant will still comply with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: 5. We recommended that the City Treasurer’s Office intensify the collection of the delinquent RPTs by conducting tax campaigns and issuing demand letters, utilizing the assistance of barangay officials in the distribution thereof. We also recommended that Management institute legal remedies as provided in the City’s Revenue Code.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer’s Office will still comply with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: 11. We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to update the Ledger Cards/Subsidiary Schedule of PPE and direct the Property and Accounting Units to reconcile the results of the inventory count with the related property and accounting records in accordance with the pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to ensure the accuracy of the reported PPE balances in the financial statements. Any discrepancies noted in the reconciliation shall be adjusted in accordance with Sections 7.0 and 8.0 of the same Circular as one-time cleansing of the PPE account balances.
observations.managementAction: The City office will still have to comply with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: 14. We recommended that Management require the concerned Barangay thru their respective Sangguniang Barangays to enact ordinances to include in their respective budgets the amounts granted to them for refund to the City; otherwise, all payments to barangay officials and employees will be disallowed in audit. Moreover, require the barangay elective officials to refund the hazard pay as they are not entitled to it.
observations.managementAction: Management require the concerned Barangay thru their respective Sangguniang Barangays to enact ordinances to include in their respective budgets the amounts granted to them; otherwise, all payments to barangay officials and employees will be disallowed in audit. Moreover, require the barangay elective officials to refund the hazard pay as they are not entitled to it.

observations.recommendation:
15. We recommended that the DRRM Council, through the City Mayor as Chairperson, strictly comply with existing regulations on the appropriation and utilization of LDRRMF and ensure that such funds are utilized solely for disaster risk management, making the City well-prepared to respond to calamities which may occur. We further recommended that the procurement of diesel and gasoline fuel, meals for frontliners and supplies used for the operation of the Incident Command Post shall be sourced from the General Fund of the LGU and refund the LDRRMF for the amount expended.

16. We further recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to include in the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF balances of prior years and to submit the same as required under Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002. Likewise, we recommended that the City Accountant determine and address the cause/s of the discrepancy in reporting the unutilized balance of LDRRMF between the LDRRMF Utilization Report and the Statement of Financial Position.
observations.managementAction:
Relief goods are now properly accounted.

The City will still comply with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: 16. We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to include in the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF balances of prior years and to submit the same as required under Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002. Likewise, we recommended that the City Accountant determine and address the cause/s of the discrepancy in reporting the unutilized balance of LDRRMF between the LDRRMF Utilization Report and the Statement of Financial Position.
observations.managementAction: The City will still comply with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: 18. We recommended that the City Accountant demand from the former City Mayor the immediate settlement/ liquidation of the unliquidated cash advances for confidential and intelligence expenses and follow up with the Intelligence and Confidential Funds Audit Unit of the COA the credit notice for liquidation reports already submitted.
observations.managementAction: In line with the recommendation of the Commission on Audit, a formal demand letter was issued to the former Local Chief Executive (LCE), requiring the immediate settlement or liquidation of the said confidential and intelligence cash advances. Management remains committed to ensuring accountability and is undertaking all necessary steps to resolve the matter in compliance with applicable rules and regulations governing the use of confidential and intelligence funds.

observations.recommendation: 19. We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to assign the different bookkeeping jobs not just to one employee but to several employees to hasten the recording of transactions and preparation of financial statements.
observations.managementAction: In response to the audit recommendation, the City Office has since distributed the bookkeeping responsibilities among several accounting personnel. The adoption of the eNGAS is anticipated to further streamline accounting processes and strengthen internal controls through digital recordkeeping and automation of accounting functions. Coordination with COA's ASDOSO regarding implementation of eNGAS is ongoing.

observations.recommendation:
20. The City Mayor designate one or two personnel to focus on the monitoring and collection of the livelihood development loans, to issue demand letters within a reasonable time after due dates when no payment has been received, and to refer to the City Legal Officer those who refuse to pay for the purpose of instituting legal action pursuant to the terms of the loan agreement.

21. The City Cooperative Officer conduct proper orientation of all loan applicants before the loans are granted in order to instill in their minds that the amounts granted are loans and not dole-outs, thus subject to repayment and that the City Government can file charges against those who fail to pay.
observations.managementAction: The COOP Office has taken action by issuing formal collection letters to loan recipients with outstanding balances. The CCO is conducting orientations for loan applicants. Proper orientation was conducted to loan applicants.

observations.recommendation:
22. We recommended that the LPRAT, through the BUB Focal Person, intensify efforts to fast-track the implementation of the BUB program for the LGU to achieve its target of reducing poverty and likewise avoid any repercussions that may result from its non-utilization of BUB Funds as well as delayed implementation of related programs/projects by assigning technical personnel who will focus on BUB projects and by closely monitoring progress thereon.

Note: CY 2016 – Four projects totaling ₱4,610,000.00.
observations.managementAction: In response to the audit observation, the LGU, through the BUB Focal Person, will intensify efforts to fast-track the implementation of the remaining BUB projects by assigning dedicated personnel and strengthening coordination with implementing agencies. Dedicated technical staff have been assigned to oversee BUB projects, with strengthened coordination and regular monitoring now in place to promptly address bottlenecks.

observations.recommendation: 23. We recommended that Management secure from the owner of the private lot the Transfer Certificate of Title (TCT) and Deed of Donation in favor of the government, if any, otherwise, initiate expropriation proceedings before the proper court to acquire the land upon payment of just compensation to the owner. Also, direct them to process the titling of the lots owned by the City and for the City Budget Officer to allocate funds for the titling thereof. Likewise instruct the City Accountant to review the land account and record in the books of accounts those lots that are owned by the City. Moreover, the value of the lots which will be determined by the City Appraisal Committee shall also be recorded in the books of accounts to avoid understatement of assets.
observations.managementAction: Management has still to comply with the recommendation. The City has to secure from the private lot owner the Transfer Certificate of Title (TCT) and Deed of Donation in favor of the government, or else initiate expropriation proceedings. Management will coordinate with the City Legal Officer to pursue expropriation proceedings and represent the LGU in court. The City Budget Officer will allocate funds for titling once required actions are initiated.

observations.recommendation: 24. We recommended that the City record separately the transactions of the waterworks system and cemetery operations by maintaining special accounts in the General Fund in accordance with Section 313 of RA No. 7160 and Sections 105 and 107 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: Management has still to conduct a study if the waterworks and cemetery operation will qualify for a special account in the General Fund.

observations.recommendation:
25. We recommended that the City Accountant maintain PPE Ledger Cards to facilitate the computation of depreciation expenses and henceforth compute and record, at the end of the year, depreciation of properties whose costs and acquisition dates are available.

26. The City Accountant trace the cost of depreciable immovable properties and take up current and prior years’ depreciation expense in order to reflect the correct balance of PPE in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Management has still to comply with the recommendation. The City Accountant has already initiated the proper recording of depreciation expenses for items with available cost and acquisition dates. However, depreciation for pre-2022 PPEs has been delayed due to missing or incomplete records. Efforts are ongoing to recover records and compute retrospective depreciation where supported.

observations.recommendation:
27. We recommended that Management require the City Assessor, as well as the City Treasurer, to convene the City Appraisal Committee to act immediately in the determination of the value of the unrecorded lots.

28. Also, direct them to process the titling of the lots owned by the City and for the City Budget Officer to allocate funds for the titling thereof. Likewise, instruct the City Accountant to review the land account and record in the books of accounts those lots that are owned by the City. Moreover, the value of the lots which will be determined by the City Appraisal Committee shall also be recorded in the books of accounts to avoid understatement of assets. However, attention is also directed pertaining to the recording of the value of the lot as provided in Paragraph 46 of the IPSAS (Ba), Volume II and Paragraph 7 of the same regulation.
observations.managementAction: Management has still to comply with the recommendation. The Assessor, City Treasurer and other concerned offices have been tasked to convene the City Appraisal Committee to determine values, process titling, and allocate funds necessary for titling. The City Accountant has been directed to review the land account and ensure lots owned by the City are recorded. Coordination with Legal Officer will be pursued for expropriation when necessary.

observations.recommendation: 29. To protect the interest of the City, we recommended that Management facilitate the transfer of the ownership of the 10,066 square meters of the lot purchased as soon as possible, otherwise the same shall be disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: In response to the Assessor's recommendation, the City has taken steps to rectify the issue. The City Assessor has been tasked to lead and facilitate the processing and transfer of ownership of the subject property. Coordination with the original owner, the Registry of Deeds, and other relevant government agencies is currently ongoing to secure and register the Certificate of Title in the name of the City of Canlaon.

observations.recommendation: 30. We recommended that Management request the City Legal Officer to communicate with the sponsoring organization demanding the refund of the advance payment of registration fees for the Conference on City Development Asia 2007 in Singapore, otherwise, require the officials concerned to refund the amount so paid.
observations.managementAction: In response to the Commission on Audit’s recommendation, Management is currently in the process of referring the matter to the City Legal Officer and preparing the necessary documents for endorsement. The City Legal Officer will be requested to formally communicate with the sponsoring organization and initiate demand for refund.

observations.recommendation:
1.21 We recommended and Management agreed to observe the guidelines and
procedures provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020,
for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances to have reliable PPE
balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition, and accountability as
follows:

a. Strictly follow the guidelines and procedures in the conduct of the physical
count of PPE, recognition of PPE items found at the station, and disposition
of non-existing or missing PPE items, and require the Accountant and
Property Officer to reconcile their records based on the results of the actual
physical inventory and effect necessary adjustments;
b. Adopt the uniform numbering system for property numbers of PPE and
require the Property Unit to update the property stickers based on the
prescribed format and to include the required information under Sections
5.6 and 5.7 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020;
c. Submit the approved PIP to the COA Audit Team within the prescribed
period;
d. Conduct the required preliminary activities prior to inventory taking;
e. Record or document the physical count daily in a standard ICF prescribed
in Annex A of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020; and
f. Require the Accounting and Property Units to maintain or update the
respective PPELCs and PCs and undertake collaborative procedures to
ensure that all PPEs in the RPCPPE are duly recorded in their respective
records and that the PCs maintained by the Property Unit and the PPELCs
maintained by the Accounting Unit are reconciled.

2.13 We recommended and Management agreed to enjoin the concerned
department heads to strictly comply with the accounting and reporting
guidelines on the local roads asset management system and earnestly perform
their respective duties and responsibilities set forth under COA Circular No.
2015-008 dated October 23, 2015.

2.14 We further recommended and the Inventory Committee through the General
Services Offices agreed to conduct an annual physical count of all LRN and
report the results thereof in the RPCLRN to be submitted to the Auditor and
Accounting Unit not later than January 31 of each year. The RPCLRN shall
be reconciled with the accounting records, and the necessary adjusting entries
will be prepared before the statement date.

3.12 We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to record the transfer of
the various completed projects under the Trust Funds-LGSF to the General
Fund and compute the corresponding depreciation expenses for these PPE
accounts.

4.10 We recommended and Management agreed to prepare a correcting entry
debiting Cash in Bank-Local Currency, Time Deposits, and crediting Cash in
Bank-Local Currency Savings Account amounting to ₱250 million for the total
amount placed under time deposits and direct the City Accountant to present
investments in Time Deposits with maturities of 90 days or less amounting to
₱200 million as Cash and Cash Equivalents while account with a maturity
term of more than 91 days as Investments in accordance with IPSAS 2.

5.15 We recommended and the City Treasurer agreed to provide the City
Accountant with a copy of the RPT records for CY 2023 and prior years to
establish the beginning balance for CY 2024. Henceforth, furnish the City
Accountant at the beginning of each year a certified list of taxpayers with the
amount due and collectible for the current year as the basis for the recording of
the RPT and SET receivables in compliance with Section 20 of the NGAS
Manual for LGUs, Volume I.

5.16 We further recommended and the City Accountant agreed to maintain RPT
and SET receivables subsidiary ledgers and reconcile the accounts with the
City Treasurer’s records.

6.6. We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to prepare the Monthly
Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF with the required information
and submit the report on or before the 15th day after the end of each month
through the LDRRMO to the COA Auditor.

7.5. We recommended and the City Accounting Office agreed to ensure that the
net take-home pay of employees shall not be less than ₱5,000.00 by prioritizing
deductions in the order of preference mandated under Section 55 of the 2023
GAA.

8.11. We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to ensure that the
monetization of 50 per cent of leave credits is properly supported with valid
documents to justify the grant in compliance with Section 23 of the Omnibus
Rules on Leave and COA Circular No. 2012-001, before processing the
payment.

8.12. We further recommended and Management agreed that leave credits be
monetized only once a year pursuant to CSC and DBM Joint Circular No. 2-
97.

9.8. We recommended and Management agreed to revisit the computation of the
AOs' accountabilities and increase their fidelity bond in accordance with
Treasury Circular No. 1-2022 dated May 30, 2022.

10.7 We recommended and Management agreed to formulate an action plan to
implement the audit recommendations and submit immediately the duly
accomplished AAPSI form to the Audit Team, copy furnished to the DBM, the
House Committee on Appropriations, and the Senate Committee on Finance,
as mandated by Section 99 of RA No. 11936.

10. We recommended that Management desist from engaging the legal services of private lawyers unless prior approval from the Solicitor General and written concurrence of COA are first secured. In the absence of a City Legal Officer, the LGU may instead secure the services of the Provincial Legal Officer.
observations.managementAction: The City had stopped engaging the legal services of private lawyers.

observations.recommendation:
12. Strictly comply with the documentary requirements for the transfer of funds to barangay as the implementing agencies of government programs and projects pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and Item 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

13. Record fund transfers to barangays were already recorded under Due from LGUs account instead of Subsidy to the LGUs for easy monitoring thereof.
observations.managementAction:
The City Accountant complied with the recommendation.

Due from LGU account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor and HR assess the necessity of hiring job order workers versus filling vacant regular positions and ensure jobs are not duplicative of regular functions.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed to assess and limit hiring of job orders and to prioritize filling regular positions where necessary.
observations.reason: Job orders were still hired to perform clerical and administrative functions; full shift to regular hires not yet accomplished.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City record separately the transactions of the waterworks system and cemetery by maintaining special accounts in the General Fund in accordance with Section 313 of RA 7160, Sections 105 and 107 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs.
observations.managementAction: The City will maintain special accounts for Waterworks and Cemetery Operations in the General Fund as required.
observations.reason: Management has yet to fully implement separate special accounts for these economic enterprises.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to facilitate the transfer of ownership of the subject lot as soon as possible otherwise the same shall be disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: The City will process the transfer of ownership of the 10,066 square meters of the lot purchased as soon as possible and will demand refund if documentation cannot be produced.
observations.reason: Lack of documents to process the transfer of the subject lot; ownership/titling not yet completed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to request the City Legal Officer to communicate with the sponsoring organization demanding the refund of the advance payment of registration fees for the conference or require the sponsoring organization to refund the amount; otherwise require the concerned officials to refund.
observations.managementAction: Request to sponsoring organization to refund the advance payments was made; sponsoring organization could not be located or contacted.
observations.reason: The sponsoring organization could no longer be located or contacted; refund not obtained.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to
update the Ledger Cards/Subsidiary Schedule of PPE and direct the Property
and Accounting Units to reconcile the results of the inventory count with the
related property and accounting records in accordance with the pertinent
provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006, dated January 31, 2020, to ensure
the accuracy of the reported PPE balances in the financial statements. Any
discrepancies noted in the reconciliation shall be adjusted in accordance with
Section 7.0 and 8.0 of the same Circular as one-time cleansing of the PPE
account balances.

We recommended that the City Accountant:
a. Strictly comply with the documentary requirements for the transfer of
funds to barangay as implementing agencies of government programs and
projects pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and Item 3.1.1 of COA
Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.
b. Record fund transfers to barangays under Due from LGUs account instead
of Subsidy to Local Government Units for easy monitoring thereof.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the concerned
Barangays thru their respective Sangguniang Barangays to enact ordinances
to include the hazard pay in their respective budgets and to refund the City
Government the amounts granted to them; otherwise, all payments to
barangay officials and employees will be disallowed in audit. Moreover,
require the barangay elective officials to refund the hazard pay as they are not
entitled to it.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City Accountant
exercise diligence in the recording of transactions so as to ensure that all
transactions are recorded correctly. Moreover, they also agreed that a Journal
Entry Voucher taking up the unrecorded collections amounting to ₱69,891.53
be drawn immediately in the Trust Fund books.

We recommended that the City Accountant fast-track the preparation
of the financial statements and to closely monitor strict adherence to the
requirements of pertinent laws and regulations relating to the submission of
financial statements and other accounting reports as well as the disbursement
vouchers with complete documentation.

We recommended and Management agreed to post the Report on Fund
Utilization and Status of Implementation of PPAs under the BGCM in the
LGU’s website and in at least three conspicuous public places in the locality at
the end of every month in compliance with Items 4.2 and 4.3 of DBM LBC No.
125 for purposes of transparency, monitoring and evaluation of the LGU’s
response and measure related to the COVID-19 emergency.

We recommended that Management maximize the utilization of the
20% DF so as not to deprive the constituents and the City itself of the benefits
which could be derived from the full implementation of the funded projects.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Council, through the City Mayor as Chairman,
strictly comply with existing regulations on the appropriation and utilization
of LDRRM funds, ensuring that such funds are utilized solely for disaster risk
management, making the City well-prepared to respond to calamities which
may occur. We further recommended and Management also agreed that the
procurement of diesel and gasoline fuel, meals for frontliners and supplies
used for the operation of the Incident Command Post shall be sourced from
the General Fund of the LGU and refund the LDRRMF for the amount
expended.

We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the City
Accountant to include in the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of
LDRRMF balances of prior years and to submit the same as required under
Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002. Likewise, we also recommended
and the City Accountant agreed to determine and address the cause/s of the
discrepancy in reporting the unutilized balance of LDRRMF between the
LDRRMF Utilization Report and the Statement of Financial Position.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Bids and Awards
Committee in consultation with the end user ensure that technical specifications
of the items to be procured be provided in the bidding documents to facilitate
competitive bidding and to fill up the relevant and required information in the
PO pursuant to Item B of COA Circular No. 96-010 dated August 15, 1996.
We also recommended and Management also agreed that the BAC should
refrain from awarding a contract unless the RFQ contains the required details
of the goods procured.

We recommended and Management agreed that henceforth, the City
School Board ensure that charges to the SEF shall only be to those allowed by
law and regulations in order that the purposes for which the fund was
established will be attained.
We recommended that Management require the City Accountant and the City Budget Officer to record the amount of expenses of the City to its appropriate specific account.
observations.managementAction: Transactions were recorded to the appropriate specific account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the Inventory Committee to coordinate with the Accounting Office for the reconciliation of the inventory reports and accounting records in order to establish the correctness of the recorded value of PPE accounts.
observations.managementAction: Committee conducted the physical count of movable property.
observations.reason: Delayed completion of the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) for CY 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer require the collectors to remit their collections daily and intact to the Liquidating Officer in accordance with Section 63 of the GAM for LGUs, to safeguard government funds against loss or misappropriation.
observations.managementAction: Collections were remitted intact to the Liquidating Officers at the close of each business day.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management require the requisitioner of the food supplies purchased to submit the daily menu for the meals served for persons under monitoring to the Audit Team and further recommended that RDS be submitted to account for the relief goods purchased.
observations.managementAction: Encoding of data from the relief distributions and keeping stock cards to ensure that relief goods are properly accounted for is still ongoing.
observations.reason: Delayed completion of the Report on the Physical Count/encoding and submission of RDS to account for distributed relief goods.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the requisitioner of the food supplies purchased to submit the daily menu for the meals served for persons under monitoring to the Audit Team for validation. Moreover, we recommended that the end user and the BAC adhere strictly to Section 7.3.2 (f) of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: Menus of meals served to PUMs were prepared and submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management re-align, through a Sangguniang Panlungsod resolution, ineligible projects which were not yet implemented and refund those that are not feasible.
observations.managementAction: Construction of administrative office/building is no longer charged against the 20% DF under DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 dated November 4, 2020. Ineligible projects will be refunded by the General Fund. Management will re-align projects as recommended.
observations.reason: There were still ineligible projects charged against 20% DF which were later addressed; further alignment and refund actions ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the 20% GAD funds not be utilized for payment of wages of job order workers whose functions do not clearly address GAD-related PPAs and that the LGU ensure PPAs in the GAD Plan and Budget are aligned with GAD-related undertakings.
observations.managementAction: Wages of literacy teachers are no longer charged to the GAD Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the procurement of supplies and equipment shall be sourced from the General Fund of the LGU and refund the LDRRMF for the amount expended. Further, food expenses to frontliners involved in COVID-19 related PPAs and related expenses shall be charged to BGCM and 20% DF throughout the duration of the State of Calamity.
observations.managementAction: Some of the COVID related PPAs were charged to the LDRRMF in 20% CY 2021. Management agreed to refund the LDRRMF and to charge appropriate expenses to BGCM and 20% DF as appropriate.
observations.reason: Some items charged to LDRRMF have been identified; refund/realignment actions are in process.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC ensure that the prices of procured medical supplies are not above the suggested retail price stated on the DOH Circular and procure medical supplies at reasonable prices guided by the SRP set by appropriate government agencies.
observations.managementAction: The BAC ensured that the procured medical supplies are not above the suggested retail price of the DOH. Henceforth, procure medical supplies at reasonable prices guided by SRP.
observations.reason: Some procurements were above SRP; justification and price checking actions remain to be completed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor withhold payment of any additional cash advances to officials and employees with unliquidated cash advances and demand settlement of unliquidated cash advances thirty days after demand letters where no liquidation has been submitted.
observations.managementAction: Officials and employees with unliquidated cash advances thirty days after demand were not granted additional cash advances. Demand letters were issued to the former City Mayor and concerned officials for settlement of unliquidated cash advances.
observations.reason: Large amount of unliquidated advances remain; follow-up and collection actions continue.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant demand immediate settlement/liquidation of the unliquidated confidential and intelligence advances and follow up with the Audit Unit the submission of liquidation reports.
observations.managementAction: Demand notices/settlement letters were issued to the former City Mayor. Follow up and processing of liquidation reports are ongoing.
observations.reason: Liquidation reports are still being processed and follow-up actions ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant assign the bookkeeping jobs not just to one employee but to several employees in order to hasten the recording of transactions and the preparation of financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The City assigned bookkeeping and recording tasks to several employees though preparation and submission still affected previously.
observations.reason: There was still concentration in practice; additional staffing/training needed to fully address delays.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the contractor be required to correct defects and refund the overpayment. Also, compel the contractor to fulfill its obligations and apply appropriate sanctions. Recover incorrect VAT application from the contractor.
observations.managementAction: The City is now operational; the incorrect application of VAT was paid to the BIR and not to the contractor. Contractual and corrective actions were undertaken.
observations.reason: Verification and finalization of adjustments and recoveries are still ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor designate personnel to focus on monitoring and collection of the loans, issue demand letters, refer delinquent accounts to the City Legal Officer for legal action, and conduct proper orientation of loan applicants.
observations.managementAction: Delinquent borrowers were not allowed to avail new loans and the City will review the accounts and issue demand letters. Orientation measures were recommended.
observations.reason: Delinquent accounts still exist; collection and legal referral efforts are ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City discontinue such support until a law is passed authorizing the same and that City officials involved submit lists of recipients duly acknowledged.
observations.managementAction: Lists of recipients of the food commodities distributed in CY 2018 were submitted to the audit team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LPRAT, through the BUB Focal Person, intensify efforts to fast-track implementation of the BUB programs and assign technical personnel to focus on BUB projects and monitoring.
observations.managementAction: The Level III water system of the City is now operational. The LGU assigned personnel to focus on BUB projects and monitoring.
observations.reason: Some BUB projects implemented; others delayed due to COVID-19 and capacity/technical constraints.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management secure from the owner of the private lot the Deed of Donation in favor of the government or initiate expropriation proceedings and that Property and General Services and Accounting Offices maintain property and ledger cards in accordance with Section 114 of the NGAS Manual.
observations.managementAction: Submitted request to SP for Authority and Ordinance to expropriate the 18-hectare barangay lot; appraisal actions were initiated.
observations.reason: LGU's offer to purchase was rejected by PNB; titling and documentation actions remain pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management review all balances specifically those dormant for two years to more than 10 years and identify unutilized or unimplemented accounts and return dormant trust fund accounts and excess funds to the general fund and source agency as appropriate.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant will review the balances and return the unexpended balances once demand letters from source agencies are received.
observations.reason: The City Accountant is hesitant to return funds to source agencies without proper demand letters/documentation; action pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management require the retired employee to refund the total amount representing the illegal payments otherwise the approving authority shall be held responsible; and that the LGU strictly follow IRR provisions of R.A. No.10154.

observations.reason: Notices of Disallowance were issued and collection actions started; full recovery not yet completed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management require all city officials and employees to refund the overpayment of PEI and to observe strictly the limitations on the grant of PEI.
observations.managementAction: Monthly payroll deductions were made and amounts were collected to refund the disallowed amount. Notices of Disallowance were issued to monitor settlement.
observations.reason: Total amount collected as of 12/31/2021 amounted to ₱5,421,810.24 bringing the balance of disallowed amount to ₱3,160,789.76.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management compute and record, at the end of the year, depreciation of properties whose costs and acquisition dates are available and trace cost of depreciable immovable properties and take up current and prior years’ depreciation expense to reflect correct PPE balances.
observations.managementAction: Submitted computation and will record depreciation; LGU to compute current and prior years’ depreciation where costs/acquisition dates available.
observations.reason: Cost tracing and full computation of prior years' depreciation remains in progress; verification of records required.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management require the concerned to refund the amount improperly paid and consider restoring the salary of the CDRRMO to the salary of a Project Development Officer II to avoid further disallowance in audit.
observations.managementAction: The creation of the position of the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office is already allowed under NDRRMC, DILG, DBM and CSC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-01 dated April 4, 2014. Notice of Disallowance was issued in CY 2013 to monitor settlement.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management convene the City Appraisal Committee to act immediately in the determination of the value of the unrecorded lots; process the titling of lots owned by the city and have the City Accountant review and record land accounts in the books.
observations.managementAction: Submitted request to SP for authority; City Appraisal Committee to process valuation and titling. City Budget Officer to allocate funds for titling.
observations.reason: LGU has not prioritized composition/valuation and titling actions; some steps initiated but not completed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor and the City Human Resource and Management Officer carefully assess the necessity of hiring job order workers as against filling up available vacant regular positions and ensure job order functions do not duplicate existing regular employees’ functions.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed to assess the necessity of hiring job order workers and ensure that their functions do not duplicate those of existing regular employees.
observations.reason: Job Orders were still hired to perform clerical and administrative functions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management maintain separate special accounts for the waterworks system and cemetery operations and convene for procedural compliance.
observations.managementAction: Management indicated that records for Waterworks and Cemetery Operations were being prepared and that issues to create separate special accounts were being addressed.
observations.reason: Management still to convene the Waterworks and Cemetery Operations administrative procedures to effect separation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management compute and record depreciation for properties whose costs and acquisition dates are available and trace costs of depreciable immovable properties and take up current and prior years’ depreciation expense to reflect correct balances.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed to compute and record depreciation for properties whose costs and acquisition dates are available and to compute depreciation at year-end.
observations.reason: Computation and recording of depreciation for many properties still pending; verification and records incomplete.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management facilitate the transfer of ownership of the 10,066 square meters of the lot purchased as soon as possible, otherwise the same shall be disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: Efforts to process transfer of ownership were undertaken but documents were lacking; Management to process transfer as soon as possible.
observations.reason: Lack of documents to process the titling of the lot.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Legal Officer communicate with the sponsoring organization to demand refund of the advance payment or require the officials concerned to refund the amount if sponsor cannot be located.
observations.managementAction: Attempts were made to communicate with sponsoring organization demanding refund; however, the sponsoring organization could no longer be located or contacted.
observations.reason: The sponsoring organization could no longer be located or contacted; documents not available for follow-up.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the City Accountant and the City Budget
Officer to record the expenses of the City to its appropriate specific account provided
under COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 01, 2015 so that a more precise
budgetary control could be put in place and proper recording could be made.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Inventory
Committee to coordinate with the Accounting Office for the reconciliation of
the inventory reports and accounting records in order to establish the
correctness of the recorded value of PPE accounts.

We recommended that the City Treasurer require the collectors to
remit their collections daily and intact to the Liquidation Officer in
accordance with Section 63 of the GAM, to safeguard government funds
against loss or misappropriation.

We recommended that Management install internal control
mechanisms by using the RIS and keeping stock cards to ensure that relief
goods are properly accounted for and safeguarded from possible theft, loss and wastage. We further recommended that RDS be submitted to account for the relief goods purchased.

We recommended that Management require the requisitioner of the
food supplies purchased to submit the daily menu for meals served for persons
under monitoring to the Audit Team for validation. Moreover, we
recommended that the end user and the BAC adhere strictly to Section 7.3.2
(f) of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the provisions
of DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February
22, 2017 in the utilization of the 20% DF. We further recommended that
expenditures for ineligible projects shall be refunded by the General Fund
Proper. We also recommended that Management re-align, through a
Sangguniang Panlungsod resolution, ineligible projects which were not yet
implemented.

We recommended that the Management stop the utilization of GAD
funds for the payment of wages of job order workers unless they are hired to
assist in GAD-related PPAs and henceforth ensure that the PPAs in the GAD
Plan and Budget are aligned with GAD-related undertakings so that the
intended beneficiaries are assured of the services, protection and other
assistance that may be provided.

We recommended that the procurement of supplies and equipment
shall be sourced from the General Fund of the LGU and refund the LDRRMF
for the amount expended. Further, we recommended that food expenses to
frontliners directly involved in the implementation of COVID-19 related
PPAs, and other related expenses shall be charged to BGCM and 20% DF
throughout the duration of the State of Calamity as declared by the President
through Proclamation No. 929. Henceforth, the LDRRMF shall be utilized for
PPAs stipulated in the NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum
Circular No. 2013-01 dated March 25, 2013 to strengthen the capacity of the
City to avoid or mitigate the adverse effects of disasters or calamity.

We recommended that Management require the Bids and Awards
Committee to submit justification to prove reasonableness for the
procurement of the aforecited items above the suggested price of the DOH.
Henceforth, procure medical supplies at reasonable prices guided by the SRP
set by appropriate government agencies to ensure procurement that is most
advantageous to the government.

We recommended that the City Mayor and officials desist from granting additional cash advances and that withholding of the payment of any cash money due to officials and employees with unliquidated cash advances be ordered after the service of demand letters pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002.
observations.managementAction: Officials and the City Mayor agreed to order the withholding of the payment of any cash money due to officials and employees with unliquidated cash advances thirty days after the service of demand letters by the accountable officers concerned. Management also agreed that henceforth, they will desist from granting additional cash advances.
observations.reason: Non-issuance of demand letter during the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant demand settlement of unliquidated advances from the former City Mayor and that the Intelligence and Confidential Funds Audit Unit of the COA be furnished with the necessary reports.
observations.managementAction: Demand on the former City Mayor for the immediate settlement/liquidation of the unliquidated cash advances for confidential and intelligence expenses was issued. Liquidation reports are being processed and credit notice for liquidation reports already submitted.
observations.reason: Liquidation reports are still being processed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that cash assistance to indigents be supported with the required documents under DSWD Administrative Order No. 5, s. of 2008 as amended by DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 11, s. of 2019 and that the utilization of GAD funds for payment of cash assistance to indigents be stopped.
observations.managementAction: Assistance under the Aid in Crisis Situation (AICS) Program of the DSWD were supported by the documents required under DSWD Administrative Order No. 5, s. of 2008 as amended by DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 11, s. of 2019. Management agreed that henceforth, payments of cash assistance to indigents will be supported by the required documents.
observations.reason: Liquidation reports are being processed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City require all PHWs to support their claims for hazard pay with proof of actual exposure to specific health hazards and occupational risks and with certifications from appropriate agencies and documents required under Section 5.8 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 in order to establish validity of payments.
observations.managementAction: The City and Management agreed that the City Accountant require all PHWs to support their claims for hazard pay with proof of actual exposure to specific health hazards and occupational risks and with certifications from appropriate agencies and the documents required under Section 5.8 of COA Circular No. 2012-001.
observations.reason: Declaration of actual exposure to specific health hazards with certifications and documents required were submitted and the supporting documents were approved.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee desist from processing and awarding contracts for infrastructure projects unless the supporting documents required under R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR are submitted, and that required documents be submitted to facilitate review and audit of the propriety and reasonableness of amounts paid.
observations.managementAction: The City Engineer and Management agreed to immediately submit the required documents and facilitate review and audit of the propriety and reasonableness of the amounts paid.
observations.reason: The required documents were submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant assign the bookkeeping jobs not just to one employee but to several employees in order to hasten the recording of transactions and preparation of financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant assigned bookkeeping and preparation of financial statements to several employees to hasten the recording of transactions and preparation of financial statements.
observations.reason: Recording of transactions and preparation of financial statements for General Fund and Trust Fund were still assigned to one employee.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Assessor submit a Revised Schedule of Market Value (SMV) and that the SMV be endorsed to the Sangguniang Panlungsod for the passage of an Ordinance incorporating therein the proposed SMV as basis for the conduct of a general revision of real property assessments.
observations.managementAction: BLGF-approved Schedule of Market Value was endorsed to SP on January 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the contractor rectify the defects until the system operates normally and refund the overpayment, and that Management compel the contractor to fulfill its obligations and apply sanctions where appropriate.
observations.managementAction: Processing of clearance from said new water Board (PAMB) for the new water source is ongoing. The defects have been rectified by the contractor.
observations.reason: Processing of clearance for the new water source is ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management fast-track the establishment of the GAD Database and disaggregate data collected to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning and programming.
observations.managementAction: The Local Chief Executive sent a letter of intent/offer to the Philippine National Bank to buy the subject property. (Note: This appears to be misaligned in the table; however, for the GAD Database, Management stated they would create and database and disaggregate data collected.)
observations.reason: The lists of recipients of the food commodities that were distributed in CY 2018 were already submitted to the audit team. (Note: table shows partial actions and submissions.)

observations.recommendation: We strongly recommended that Management take immediate action on the completion and reconciliation of the inventory count with accounting records in order to fairly present the PPE account balances in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Management will review the balances and will return balances identified as unexpended/unimplemented. Reconciliation of the inventory count with accounting records was undertaken and Inventory Report submissions were made for Machinery and Equipment, Furniture, Fixtures and Books for the 28 offices of the City; Inventory Report for Transportation Equipment was submitted on February 18, 2021 and April 24, 2021 respectively.
observations.reason: Reconciliation of the inventory count with accounting records was not done completely; Inventory Report for some accounts submitted in 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management take steps to collect delinquent loans including issuing demand letters, convening collection actions, orienting loan applicants on repayment obligations, and referring defaulters to the City Legal Officer for legal action where necessary.
observations.managementAction: Demand on delinquent borrowers was made; the City Cooperative Officer conducts orientation of borrowers; Management will follow up and refer to Legal Officer those who refuse to pay.
observations.reason: Non-issuance of demand letters to delinquent borrowers during the year due to focus on COVID-19 pandemic.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management discontinue provision of food to military detachments and CAFGUs unless a law is passed authorizing the same, and require City officials who were involved in the distribution to submit lists of recipients duly acknowledged.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed to discontinue such support until a law is passed authorizing the same. For the food commodity assistance already distributed, City officials involved in the distribution submitted lists of recipients duly acknowledged.
observations.reason: The list of recipients already submitted to the audit team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management ensure detailed engineering activities and preparation of required procurement documents for all infrastructure projects to avoid technical problems and ensure project feasibility and sufficiency of appropriations.
observations.managementAction: Detailed engineering activities for the remaining projects in the previous years were conducted and projects with no longer feasible development were realigned to other projects. Management also instituted measures to ensure completeness of documents.
observations.reason: Some projects were realigned; others still faced procurement and technical issues delaying implementation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LPRAT, through the BUB Focal Person, intensify efforts to fast-track the implementation of the BUB program and assign technical personnel to focus on BUB projects and closely monitor progress.
observations.managementAction: Management intensified efforts to fast-track implementation of BUB projects and assigned technical personnel to monitor and implement the programs.
observations.reason: Some activities were deferred due to COVID-19 pandemic; one project remained unfinished due to capacity constraints.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management create a Disposal Committee who shall take charge of the disposal of unserviceable properties following existing guidelines such as COA Circular No. 92-386.
observations.managementAction: Appraisal and Disposal Committee has been created to take charge of the disposal of the unserviceable properties, following existing guidelines.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management give priority to insurable properties, retrieve building plans needed for filing insurance applications, appropriate necessary premiums in the annual budget and insure the properties with the GSIS.
observations.managementAction: Properties owned by the City were insured with the Government Insurance Fund of the GSIS.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management secure from the owner of the private lot the Transfer Certificate of Title (TCT) and Deed of Donation in favor of the government, otherwise initiate expropriation proceedings and ensure property and ledger cards are maintained.
observations.managementAction: The Local Chief Executive sent a letter of intent/offer to the Philippine National Bank to buy the subject property. The acquisition is currently in the process of negotiation.
observations.reason: The acquisition of the property is in the process of negotiation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management review and identify dormant trust fund accounts and return dormant trust fund accounts and excess funds to the general fund of the government and the source agency once ascertained, in compliance with Executive Order No. 431 and COA Circular No. 94-013.
observations.managementAction: Management reviewed balances and identified unexpended balances for fully implemented projects and will return dormant trust fund accounts and excess funds to the general fund and source agencies once ascertained.
observations.reason: Unavailability of supporting records to effect returns; some returns awaiting source agency acknowledgment.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the retired employee to refund the total amount of ₱122,799.28 and follow strictly the IRR of R.A. No. 10154 on responsibilities of employer-agency to retiring employees to avoid unlawful disbursements.
observations.managementAction: Demand was made to the retired employee to refund the amount representing salaries and other compensation paid to her from January 1, 2015 to August 31, 2015; the retired employee explained and refunded the amount.
observations.reason: Refund made by the retired employee; however, some procedural follow-ups remained.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management ensure limitations on the grant of PEI and to refund overpayments and ensure outstanding balances be deducted from terminal benefits of retiring employees.

observations.reason: Monthly payroll deductions were minimal; outstanding balance of disallowed amount remained being collected.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require all department heads to submit Consolidated Requisition and Issue Slips for fuel and desist from approving requisitions unless the previous purchase has been fully liquidated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management consider restoring back the salary of the CDRRMO to the salary of a Project Development Officer II to avoid further disallowance and that if the position was created improperly, refund be made.
observations.managementAction: Management indicated that the CDRRMO Office is now allowed under NDRRMC, DILG, DBM and CSC JMC No. 2014-01; however, creation subject to proper authority and guidelines.
observations.reason: Subject of disallowance is under appeal to the Commission; adjustments being coordinated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management convene the City Appraisal Committee to act immediately in the determination of the value of the unrecorded lots and to process titling of the lots owned by the city and allocate funds for titling; record the determined value in the books of accounts.
observations.managementAction: Management convened appraisal and started processes to determine the value of lots and process titling.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management formulate written policies and procedures, revamp personnel to handle the program, require confirmations of released amounts, and investigate and penalize erring personnel.
observations.managementAction: Policies and procedures were formulated and personnel revamped; remedial actions and confirmations instituted to avoid further incidences.
observations.reason: Policies and procedures formulated and personnel changes made; monitoring instituted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to order the withholding
of the payment of any money due to officials and employees with unliquidated
cash advances thirty days after the service of the demand letters where no
liquidation has been submitted by the accountable officers concerned,
pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002. We further recommended and
Management also agreed that henceforth, they will desist from granting
additional cash advances to accountable officers with unliquidated cash
advances.

We recommended that the City Accountant demand from the former
City Mayor the immediate settlement/liquidation of the unliquidated cash
advances for confidential and intelligence expenses and follow up with the
Intelligence and Confidential Funds Audit Unit of the COA the credit notice
for liquidation reports already submitted.

We recommended and Management agreed that vouchers covering cash
assistance paid under the Aid in Crisis Situation (AICS) Program of the
DSWD be supported by the documents required under DSWD Administrative
Order No. 5, series of 2008 as amended by DSWD Memorandum Circular No.
11, series of 2019. We further recommended that the utilization of GAD funds
for the payment of cash assistance to indigents be stopped.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City Accountant
require all PHWs to support their claims for hazard pay with proof of actual
exposure to specific health hazards and occupational risks with certifications
from appropriate agencies and the documents required under Section 5.8 of
COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 in order to establish validity
of payments.

We recommended and the City Engineer agreed to immediately submit
the required documents to facilitate review and audit of the propriety and
reasonableness of the amounts paid. We further recommended that the Bids
and Awards Committee desist from processing and awarding of contracts for
infrastructure projects unless the supporting documents required under R.A.
No. 9184 and its IRR are submitted.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City Accountant
assign the bookkeeping jobs not just to one employee but to several employees
in order to hasten the recording of transactions and preparation of financial
statements.

We recommended that Management require the City Assessor to
submit a Revised SMV pursuant to Section 212 of the Local Government Code
of 1991 and cause the passage of an Ordinance incorporating therein the
proposed SMV as basis for the conduct of a general revision of real property
assessments in compliance to Section 219 of the same Code and DILG-DOF
Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2010-001.

We strongly recommended and Management agreed to take immediate
action on the completion and reconciliation of the inventory count with
accounting records in order to fairly present the PPE account balances in the
financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed that:

a. The City Mayor designate one or two personnel to focus on the
monitoring and collection of the loans, to issue demand letters
within a reasonable time after due dates when no payment has been
received, and to refer to the City Legal Officer those who refuse to
pay for the purpose of instituting legal action pursuant to the terms
of the loan agreement.

b. The City Cooperative Officer conduct proper orientation of all loan
applicants before the loans are granted in order to instill in their
minds that the amounts granted are loans and not dole-outs, thus
subject to repayment and that the city government can file charges
against those who fail to pay.

We recommended and Management agreed that in view of the lack of
legal basis for the provision of food to military detachments and CAFGUs, the
City government discontinue such support until such time that a law is passed
authorizing the same. In addition, for the food commodity assistance already
distributed in CY 2018, Management also agreed that the City officials
involved in the distribution thereof submit a list of recipients, duly
acknowledged.

We recommended and Management agreed that meals and snacks
served during meetings with the local chief executive, budget hearings of the
Sangguniang Panlungsod, and meetings of the various local special bodies be
charged to appropriations for Extraordinary and Miscellaneous Expenses and
to representation allowance of the concerned officials. We further
recommended that the charging of similar expenses of the Philippine
Councilors’ League, various cooperatives, and personnel assigned at the
Livestock Auction Market be stopped for want of legal basis.

We recommended and Management agreed to invariably conduct
detailed engineering activities for all proposed infrastructure projects as
detailed in Annex “A” of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of
RA No. 9184 in order to avoid the problems encountered and to ensure that
projects are technically feasible, that quantity and cost estimates are
established to ensure sufficiency of appropriations, and that required
documents are complete in order to facilitate the procurement process.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City Agriculturist
submit to NAPC for approval the Sustainability Plan for the project variation
so that the Integrated Food Community Production Program will be fully
implemented.

as of December 31, 2017, to complete the inventory by reconciling the

in the financial statements, and to henceforth maintain the required subsidiary

d. Require the City Treasurer and City Accountant to conduct reconciliation
of records to establish a realistic balance of Receivables from the Real
Property Taxes and Special Education Taxes so that necessary adjustments in
the books of accounts can be effected; and
e. Require the City Treasurer to conduct information campaign to all real
property owners within the LGU and notify them of the risk of losing their
properties in case of failure to pay their taxes. Otherwise, civil remedies
provided in cases of non-payment on the part of the taxpayers despite
warnings, should be employed.

We recommended that management require the City Accountant to prepare
the necessary Journal Entry Voucher to recognize 50% of PI in the books and,
henceforth, to ensure that the required rate of PI recognition is complied with every
year-end until all the PIs are recorded back in the books by the end of CY 2019.

We recommended that the City Mayor and the City Human Resource
Management Officer carefully assess the necessity of hiring job order workers as
against filling up available vacant positions, and ensure that the work to be done by
these workers are not available in the LGU nor duplicate those of existing regular
employees.

We recommended that management henceforth exercise caution in the
allocation and utilization of public funds, ensuring that regulations are observed and
unnecessary and excessive uses of public funds are prevented.

We recommended that in the identification of projects to be charged to the
20% Development Fund, the guidelines and limitations under DILG-DBM Joint
Circular No. 2017-01 must be strictly observed.

We recommended that the 5% LDRRMF be utilized for its intended purpose
and expenditures, which are administrative in nature, be properly charged against
the regular budget or funds of the office concerned.

We recommended that management require the City Accountant to review
and investigate the difference by tracing the erroneous journal entries, to prepare
the necessary correcting entry to reconcile the Due To/From Other Funds accounts
in the General Fund, Special Education Fund and Trust Fund books as required in
Sections 28 and 152 of the NGAS Manual, Volume III, and to henceforth, regularly
reconcile these two reciprocal accounts to ensure the fairness of presentation of the
financial statements.

We recommended that management maintain the Petty Cash Fund under the
imprest system as follows:
a. The Disbursing Officer draw an initial cash advance to establish the Petty
Cash Fund at the beginning of the year, to be replenished as soon as
disbursements reach 75%, to be fully liquidated at the end of the year.
The amount shall be sufficient for the non-recurring, emergency and petty
expenses of the LGU for one month; and
b. Only petty expenses shall be paid from said cash advance through the use
of the PCV. Regular expenses such as rentals, subscriptions and utilities
shall not be paid from the Petty Cash Fund.

We recommended that management:
a. Discontinue the payment from city funds of additional or counterpart
honoraria to barangay tanods which is prohibited under existing laws, rules
and regulations; and
b. Instead, remit the counterpart funds to the respective barangays as Subsidy
to LGUs for additional honoraria of tanods, subject to submission of
liquidation reports to the city government.

We recommended that management:
a. Require the City Accountant to submit the appropriate documents to comply
with the requirements for the transfer of funds pursuant to COA Circular
No. 2012-001;
b. Require the accountable officer as well as the recipient schools to submit
immediately their liquidation reports with supporting documents for the
financial assistance received from the City Government; and
c. Henceforth, ensure strict compliance of the requirements set forth in the
provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that management:
a. Demand from the treasurers of the recipient barangays the issuance of the
required official receipts (Accountable Form No. 51) to support the financial
assistance given to them in order that the funds will be disbursed for official
purposes and subjected to government accounting and auditing rules and
regulations; and
b. Require the City Accountant to prepare the necessary correcting entries to
recognize account Due from LGUs for the financial assistance released to
the 12 barangays and to instruct the recipient barangays to submit the
liquidation reports as basis for closing the inter-agency receivable account
and, henceforth, to record financial assistance/subsidy to barangays as
receivables in the books of the city and as trust liability in the books of the
barangay.

We recommended that management improve the implementation of Solid
Waste Management pursuant to R.A. 9003 through the following:
a. Adopt a viable and effective collection schedule for specific types of wastes to
be collected;
b. Acquire additional garbage trucks to replace the dilapidated ones to
implement waste segregation at source and point of collection;
c. Widen the coverage of the city’s garbage collection system; and
d. Require the SWM Board to strengthen their Information, Education and
Communication (IEC) Program to increase the awareness of the constituents
on waste management.

We recommended that management assign additional personnel to assist the
accounting staff incharge of the preparation of the city’s financial statements and
monitor and ensure the timely preparation and submission of the required
transaction documents and financial statements/reports within their respective
reglementary periods.

We recommended that the use of the rescue vehicles for ordinary day-to-day
operations be immediately stopped in order to preserve the utility of these vehicles
to times of actual calamity solely for rescue and response activities in conformity
with DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2014-155 dated December 17, 2014.
Otherwise, if the LGU would wish to continue their present use, the LDRRMF
should be reimbursed for the cost thereof.

We recommend that management: a. Require the Inventory Committee to complete the physical count of all properties; b. Require the City Accountant and General Services Officer to maintain the property ledger cards and property records, respectively, as required by existing regulations; c. Reconcile the amount and the balances of actual properties counted with the recorded amount of properties in the books of the LGU and make the necessary adjustments.
